key_insight: EVMDOT turns dynamic optimal transport into an unconstrained energetic variational problem, letting the underlying flow and energy landscape be learned directly from time-series density data.
engineering_relevance: This provides a data-driven way to reconstruct potential landscapes and transition pathways in complex dynamical systems, with potential use in physical modeling and biomedical state-transition analysis.
method: Use the flow map to enforce the continuity equation automatically
Combine least action and maximum dissipation to recover velocity fields
Apply to Fokker--Planck dynamics to infer energy and Waddington landscapes
evidence_signal: Numerical experiments and ADNI data show the method can reconstruct landscapes robustly, with data quantity partly compensating for lower data quality.
watch_item: Landscape recovery may still depend on the chosen observation window and the quality/coverage of the sampled density trajectories.
paper_title: Learning the Energy Landscapes of Dynamical Systems via Energetic Variational Optimal Transport under Data Quantity--Quality Trade-offs
